coredata.dc:title: A nanocomposite containing orange emissive quantum dots for degradation of fluorescein sodium
coredata.dc:creator: Mousavi, S. Soraya
Sajad, Batool
Yaghoubi, Sarina
coredata.dc:description: Obtaining high-efficiency photocatalytic materials responsive to visible light is crucial to eliminating organic dye pollution in industrial wastewater. The paper describes the optical characteristics of ZnO-decorated visible-light-responsive orange-emitting carbon quantum dots ZnO (ZnO/O-CQDs composites). The composite was tested for its photocatalytic capabilities through the photodegradation of fluorescein sodium, a dye. Despite its vast usage and irritant properties, this is the first report on the photodegradation of fluorescein sodium using a customized ZnO/CQDs photocatalyst. Under visible light, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency reached 96.3 % over just 40 min. We also investigated the structural and elemental composition of the ZnO/O-CQDs using TEM, FESEM, EDS, and XPS. The results confirm that O-CQDs with varying properties promote ZnO's photocatalytic activity. The degradation obeyed the pseudo-first-order kinetics model with a photocatalytic rate constant (k) of 0.082 min−1, significantly higher than that of pure ZnO (0.048 min−1).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite
Orange emission
Photocatalyst
High rate
Fluorescein sodium

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) under Grant No. 97021370.S.Soraya.Mousavi reports financial support was provided by Iran National Science Foundation.The authors extend their thanks to the Photonics laboratory of Alzahra University for enabling the completion of this research. They also appreciate the support of the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) under grant number 97021370.

The authors extend their thanks to the Photonics laboratory of Alzahra University for enabling the completion of this research. They also appreciate the support of the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) under grant number 97021370.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A), (B) FESEM micrographs of the prepared ZnO/O-CQDs with different magnifications, (c) EDS elemental mapping of the prepared nanocomposite, (inset) the weight percentages of the composite's elements., (D), (E) TEM images of ZnO/O-CQDs Nanocomposite, (F) EDS elemental distributions.

XPS spectra of the ZnO/O-CQDs composite. (A) Survey profile, (B) the high-resolution spectra of Zn 2p, (C) the high-resolution spectra of O 1s, (D) the high-resolution spectra of C 1s, (E) the high-resolution spectra of N 1s.
N2 adsorption−desorption isotherms of ZnO/O-CQDs: adsorption (blue) and Desorption
(red). The inset shows the corresponding BJH (Barret–Joyner–Halenda) pore size distribution.

(A) Calibration curve for estimating the concentration of FS, (B) Absorption spectra of FS and its degradation by various concentrations of ZnO/O-CQDs nanocomposite, (C) A comparison between the degradations of FS using ZnO, and ZnO/O-CQDs, (D) Photodegradation curves of FS in the presence of ZnO/O-CQDs with different concentrations, (E) kinetic rate constant (kr) of ZnO/O-CQDs as a function of time and concentration.
(A) Effect of various amounts of photo-catalyst on the degradation of FS after 10 min of illumination of white light, (B) Effect of initial concentrations on FS photodegradation after 10 min.
(A) UV–visible spectra of FS for various amounts of pH after 10 min of illumination of white light, (B) Effect of pH on the FS over time.
BET analysis surface area and porosity of ZnO/O-CQDs nanocomposite.
A comparison between the photocatalytic capabilities of the reported nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: A nanocomposite containing orange emissive quantum dots for degradation of fluorescein sodium

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Atomic and Molecular Physics, Faculty of Physics, Alzahra University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Atomic and Molecular Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Atomic and Molecular Physics, Faculty of Physics, Alzahra University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Obtaining high-efficiency photocatalytic materials responsive to visible light is crucial to eliminating organic dye pollution in industrial wastewater. The paper describes the optical characteristics of ZnO-decorated visible-light-responsive orange-emitting carbon quantum dots ZnO (ZnO/O-CQDs composites). The composite was tested for its photocatalytic capabilities through the photodegradation of fluorescein sodium, a dye. Despite its vast usage and irritant properties, this is the first report on the photodegradation of fluorescein sodium using a customized ZnO/CQDs photocatalyst. Under visible light, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency reached 96.3 % over just 40 min. We also investigated the structural and elemental composition of the ZnO/O-CQDs using TEM, FESEM, EDS, and XPS. The results confirm that O-CQDs with varying properties promote ZnO's photocatalytic activity. The degradation obeyed the pseudo-first-order kinetics model with a photocatalytic rate constant (k) of 0.082 min−1, significantly higher than that of pure ZnO (0.048 min−1).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The energy supply crisis and environmental pollution are two of the foremost challenges facing the world today [1,2]. Taking on the challenges, photocatalysis based on semiconductors is one of the most promising and sustainable technologies, which uses free solar energy for energy production and pollution removal. Sustainable green technology, semiconductor-based photocatalysis, converts solar energy for environmental clean-up and renewable energy production. The currently employed photocatalysts suffer from poor photoabsorption, recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, and insufficient surface catalysts [2]. Fortunately, there are various modification techniques, such as heterojunction construction [3–6], surface modification [7–9], and defect engineering [2,10]. These approaches can amend the electronic properties of photocatalytic nanomaterial. TiO2, ZnO, WO3, W18O49, MoO3, BiO2, and SnO2 are well-known metal oxides that account for photocatalysts. Zinc oxide is a promising semiconductor photocatalyst due to its low cost, readily synthetic, nontoxic, and earth-abundant nature. With a wide band gap of 3.3 eV, its optical absorption limits to the ultraviolet region [11,12]. Therefore, it cannot efficiently degrade the pollutants under a solar regime, not utilizing the entire range of wavelengths. Additionally, it also faces complications associated with semiconductor photocatalysts. One can mitigate the semiconductor photocatalyst's drawbacks by combining it with the other catalysts [13]. Nanoscale carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have the potential to bridge this divide due to their biocompatibility, stability, and cost-effectiveness. These non-metallic materials exhibit unique properties that distinguish them from other carbon allotropes, such as photoluminescence, electron mediation, aqueous solubility, up conversion, chemical inertness, and low toxicity [13]. All these features made CQDs fill the voids of semiconductor photocatalysts. The primary challenge of CQDs is the accurate tunability of their band gaps since most synthesized CQDs produce only with the defined band gap energies. CQDs with a scalable production fashion and their band gaps extending across the entire visible spectrum are excellent building blocks for all sorts of photocatalysts. To gain the optimal photocatalytic features of ZnO, we modulated its activity by introducing highly emissive CQDs. Among the full-color emissive carbon quantum dots, we exploit the benefits of the orange emissive one due to its proper band gap and high quantum yield (59 %). These kinds of carbon dots emit the orange color (O-CQDs) at the wavelength of 535 nm and offer a band gap of about 2.00 eV [14]. We tested the photocatalytic ability of the nanocomposite by using Fluorescein Sodium (FS). A diagnostic contrast agent like fluorescein sodium is commonly used in various ophthalmic procedures, including checking for corneal or vessel abnormalities. Fluorescein is also used for imaging whole anatomic structures and can even stain cellular components in immunohistochemistry [15]. FS is classified as a safe and water-soluble material. However, its usage may have some side effects. Paresthesia of lip change in taste (orally), severe eye-stinging and irritation (ophthalmic), severe nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, chest pain, and extravasation are some of its adverse effects [16–19]. Considering its widespread usage and side effects, exploiting photocatalysts for their removal from wastewater is of particular interest.

Aiming to clarify the role of CQDs in the engineering band gap of the nanocomposite, we analyzed the optical characteristics of the ZnO/O-CQDs nanocomposite using UV–visible spectroscopy; Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL). The PL wavelengths of the CQDs and nanocomposite are positively related to their number of electron-withdrawing groups (supplementary). To elucidate the role of carbon dots in the photocatalytic process, we further pursued the functional groups on the surface of the composite via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and elemental analysis using Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS). The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method based on nitrogen adsorption/desorption with a BET BELSORP Mini II at 77 K was also used to determine the specific surface area and pore size distribution.

All the ingredients were of analytical grade and supplied by Merck. (Zn (CH3COO)2·2H2O was added to the mixture of Triethylamine (TEA) and absolute ethanol under vigorous magnetic stirring to produce a 1-M solution. TEA was adjusted to zinc acetate dihydrate at a molar ratio of 2:5. After aging and calcining (at 120 °C for an hour in an oven), the powder was annealed at 800 °C for 2 h to obtain ZnO precipitates.

The fluorescent CQDs were prepared via a one-pot solvothermal process using acetic acids (AA) and o-phenylenediamine (oPD), as described by Wang [14]. The CQDs are labeled as o-CQDs due to their orange-light luminescence. During the solvothermal treatment, the o-phenylenediamine is accountable for building the structural framework of CQDs. The 0.8-ml AA was used to react with oPD (0.1 g) for preparing o-CQDs. Afterward, the solution was transferred into a 25-ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. Following the reaction, the final solution was filtered using a 0.22 microporous membrane and then purified using dialysis in ethanol.

In the last step, 0.1 gr as-synthesized ZnO nanoparticles were added into the 5 ml as-dialyzed CQDs suspension and stirred for 2 h to be well mixed. The mixture was then dried at 50 °C overnight in an oven. The obtained powder was then annealed at 150 °C for an hour.
The morphological characteristics of the composite were also investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM).

Fig. 1 (A to C) indicates the FESEM micrographs and EDS mapping for elements of the prepared nanocomposite. The pure ZnO nanoparticles are less than 40 nm in size [12]. According to the FESEM results, the prepared nanocomposites form as micrometer sheets colonies in which the individual particles experience a remarkable enhancement in their size. The TEM images exhibit that the CQDs surrounded ZnO nanoparticles like a nanometric shell (Fig. 1 (D & E)). Fig. 1C and F present the elemental distribution of the composite. Non-negligible percentages of carbon and nitrogen are other evidence for the presence of CQDs in the structure of the prepared nanocomposite.

The surface compositions and compound's chemical states can be determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Through XPS, we elucidated how additive CQDs contribute to the photocatalytic activity of ZnO nanoparticles. Fig. 2 A shows the typical XPS-wide survey spectra of ZnO/O-CQDs nanocomposite. The presence of Zn, C, N, and O is evident in nanocomposite spectra. Fig. 2B presents the high-resolution XPS spectrum of the Zn 2p peaks.

The binding energies of 1022.28 eV and 1045.38 eV are assigned to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 peaks, respectively. As the results indicate, the chemical valence of Zn at the surface of ZnO is (+2 oxidation state) [19]. The calculated difference between the Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2 binding energies is 23 eV for ZnO. The deconvoluted O 1s XPS spectrum of ZnO/O-CQDs (Fig. 2C) contains three peaks, Zn─O (531.1 eV), adsorbed oxygen (532.08 eV), and C─O─Zn (533.3 eV). Observation of C─O─Zn bonds in the O 1s spectrum implies a strong interaction at the interface between ZnO and O-CQDs, which affirms ZnO/O-CQDs composite was successfully synthesized [20–22].

According to Fig. 2C, the C1s band of O-CQDs can be deconvoluted into three peaks, relevant to C═C/C─C (284.5 eV), C─N/C─O (286.1 eV), and carboxylic O─C═O (289.1 eV). The high-resolution spectra of N 1s contain a detectable peak at 400.08 eV. The band demonstrates the presence of NH2 groups on the surface of CQDs that bind with ZnO particles. The high-resolution spectra of N 1s contain a detectable peak at 400.08 eV. The band is evidence of the NH2 groups on the surface of CQDs that bind with ZnO particles. NH2 groups and carbon elements interact with species as the electron-donating OH group content [13]. In contrast, the higher ratio of electron-withdrawing COOH groups can interact with organic dyes and enhance the photocatalytic activity of the pure ZnO.

Recent research suggests that the photocatalytic efficiency of catalysts is not solely due to changes in specific surface area, oxygen defects, or particle size. Instead, it is attributed to the pore size, and specifically for ZnO, nanoparticles with a mesoporous structure have demonstrated higher photocatalytic degradation efficiency. In conclusion, efficient catalysts should have a reasonable pore structure, be rich in oxygen defects, and have a high specific surface area [25].
In an ideal photocatalyst, tuning the band gap structure would enable the material to absorb a broad spectrum of solar radiation, have a high redox potential, and be stable [26].

Zinc oxide, a conventional photocatalyst, suffers from some drawbacks, such as low absorption and photo corrosion. Its low absorption is due to the wide band gap energy corresponding to the UV region (∼5 %) of the solar energy spectrum. Fast recombination of photo carriers decreases the photocatalytic activity of ZnO such that charge carriers cannot reach the catalyst surface. Besides, photo corrosion of ZnO results in low stability and undergoes photo corrosion. Thus, adding O-GQDs with a lower band gap of about 2 eV compared to ZnO 3.3 eV facilitated the transition of photogenerated charge carriers.

Although the O-CQDs cannot alter the PL characteristics, they reduce excitonic intensity. It occurs because of the energy level match of O-CQDs and ZnO. ZnO and O-CQDs form a type of heterojunction in which the arrangements of band positions exhibit broken-up situations. The energy level position at the conduction band (CB) bottom of ZnO is at −3.9 eV, while that of O-CQDs is at −2.1 eV.

The charge transfers such that the conduction band of ZnO is more negative than the CB of CQDs, while the valence band (VB) of O-CQDs is also higher than that of ZnO. Hence the photogenerated charge carriers transfer from CBO-CQDs to CBZnO. Simultaneously holes transfer from VBZnO to VBO-CQDs.

In this condition, electrons accumulate in the CB of ZnO, and holes cumulate in VBO-CQDs, favoring photocatalytic activity. As a sequence, efficient charge separation occurs during the photocatalytic process [27]. We evaluated the photocatalytic activity of the catalysts via the degradation of Fluorescein Sodium (FS) under the illumination of a white LED lamp (8 W). The test solution containing the FS (20 ppm) and photocatalyst (with various w/v) was allowed for the adsorption–desorption equilibrium in the dark for 40 min. The degradation efficiency of 14 % indicates that the effect of adsorption on the reduction of FS was relatively negligible. The calibration curve was derived by measuring the absorbance intensity of different concentrations of FS at 488 nm (Fig. 4A).

Fig. 4 B indicates the photodegradation of FS versus time and different amounts of the nanocomposite (ZnO/O-CQDs). For this aim, UV–Vis absorption spectra of the FS degradation versus time in the presence of ZnO and ZnO/O-CQDs were recorded. Fig. 4C compares the photocatalytic ability of ZnO and ZnO/O-CQDs for the specific times and amounts of photocatalysts. According to the results inserting O-CQDs enhances the photocatalytic activity of the ZnO remarkably. Moreover, the high amounts of electron-withdrawing groups (COOH) attached to the edges of O-CQDs enhance the interaction of anionic and di-anionic fluorescein and catalyst significantly.

The photocatalytic degradation (Ct/C0) as a function of time can be expressed as follows:
Degradation
% =
(
C 0
−
C t
)
C 0
× 100 =
(
A 0
− A
)
A 0
× 100
Where C
t
is the concentration of FS at the time t , and C
0
is the initial concentration. Similarly, A
0
and A
t
are absorbance peak intensities of dye at the initial and the time t after irradiation, respectively [28,29].

The kinetics of the photocatalytic reactions for FS can be described by the pseudo-first-order Langmuir–Hinshelwood kinetics, as follows.
Ln
(
C t
C 0
)
= Ln
(
A t
A 0
)
= −
k r
t
Where kr (min−1) is the constant reaction rate. Generally, high kr indicates further photodegradation of the dye's molecular by photocatalysts. Fig. 4C illustrates the photodegradation rates of nanocomposite calculated for different amounts of photocatalysts. The effect of catalyst amount on the photodegradation of defined FS concentration is plotted in Fig. 4A. According to the results, a high degradation percentage of 96.2 % was recorded for the highest catalyst amount after 40 min of irradiation with white light. We calculated and plotted the photodegradation rates for different concentrations of ZnO/GQDs (Fig. 4D). Additionally, we estimated the kinetic rate constant (kr) as a function of time. The highest value of kr was recorded for the highest concentrations of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs, 0.049 min−1 and 0.082 min−1, respectively. These results imply an increase in the photocatalytic activity of ZnO after inserting O-CQDs (Table 2 ).

Fig. 5 B illustrates the effect of the initial FS concentration on the removal power of the dye by using the nanocomposite. The removal percentage of the FS decreases from 96 to 80 %, with an increase in concentration from 5 to 20 ppm.

At higher FS concentrations, the composite's active sites become occupied, and the number of available active sites for adsorbing decreases. The nanocomposite has limited active sites, which would become saturated above a defined concentration of FS. Furthermore, the availability of high concentrations of dyes for oxidative reactions with the same amounts of generated active oxidizing species may be limited [30,31].

pH is an influential factor in the photocatalytic process since pH effectively alters degradation pathways. Moreover, fluorescein possesses a complex acid-base equilibrium in aqueous solutions (depending on pH) and can exist in various forms: cationic, neutral, anionic, and di-anionic. At neutral and basic pH, anionic and di-anionic forms are present, while at acidic pH, the neutral and cationic species are most abundant [30]. Thus, we experimented with three different initial pHs prepared by HCl and KOH. Increasing pH from 3 (acidic condition) to 10 (alkaline conditions) enhances the degradation percentage of dye (Fig. 6 ).

High degradation of FS in alkaline conditions in comparison with neutral or acidic conditions occurs because; Hydroxyl radical formation at high pH intensifies due to the increase in the concentration of hydroxide ions that react with holes on the nanocomposite surface and produce hydroxyl free radicals [31].

One can compare the photocatalytic activity of various nanocomposites that have similar capabilities (Table 3 ). All the synthesized nanocomposites have demonstrated a high ability to remove dye. However, there is no report available for the degradation of Fluorescein Sodium for comparison. Based on the available data, it can be concluded that the catalyst being offered exhibits a high photocatalytic activity, attributed to its engineered band gap for dye. The synthesized nanocomposite can be regarded as potential candidates for various applications in the field of environmental remediation, water purification, and wastewater treatment.

Since the modified band gap plays a vital role in the photocatalytic behavior of catalysts, the ZnO band structure was modified using an appropriate type of CQDs. Here, we introduced a nanocomposite composed of a novel kind of carbon quantum dots with improved quantum yields. The synthesized nanocomposite utilizes the advantages of both ZnO and CQDs together. Although various composites have been produced using ZnO and carbon-based extractions, the reported nanocomposite offers a high kinetic constant rate of 0.082 min−1. The nanocomposite decolorized (96.2 %) a concentrated fluorescein sodium solution (20 ppm) after 40 min under white light irradiation. The comparative photocatalytic activity of ZnO and ZnO/O-CQDs nanostructures with controlled concentration were investigated. Thus, as-prepared nanocomposite indicated a high potential for decomposing organic pollutants, such as fluorescein sodium. The photodegradation enhancement of ZnO/O-CQDs is attributed to increased light absorption and reduced charge carrier recombination (considering the PL results). Combining ZnO and O-CQD bands forms a broken heterostructure that facilitates charge carrier separation and increases their lifetime.
This work was supported by the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) under Grant No. 97021370.

S. Soraya Mousavi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Batool Sajad: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Sarina Yaghoubi: Methodology, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their thanks to the Photonics laboratory of Alzahra University for enabling the completion of this research. They also appreciate the support of the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) under grant number 97021370.
